critique.summary: The article discusses a legal request by Anthropic to remove all references to their technology from the OpenCode codebase. This includes deleting Anthropic-specific plugins, system prompt files, beta flags, and UI hints related to Anthropic's services. The changes were implemented in a pull request on GitHub, with some noted side effects such as the unintended removal of a User-Agent header for other providers and inconsistent documentation updates.
mainPoints: Anthropic requested the removal of all Anthropic-related components from OpenCode, including plugins, prompt files, and UI elements.
The pull request removed the opencode-anthropic-auth plugin, the anthropic-20250930.txt system prompt, and the claude-code-20250219 beta header flag.
Documentation was updated to remove references to Anthropic OAuth flows, but some prose inconsistencies remain.
A side effect of the removal was the silent dropping of the User-Agent header for non-OpenCode, non-Anthropic providers, which may be unintentional.
The changes were reviewed and merged with a confidence score of 3/5, indicating some minor issues to fix.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are accurate and verifiable through the referenced GitHub pull request and code changes. The removal of Anthropic-related code and documentation is clearly documented in the commit history and code diffs. The side effects and issues mentioned are consistent with the code review comments and are transparently disclosed. There is no indication of misinformation or fabrication in the article.
considerations: The article does not mention the reasons behind Anthropic's legal request, such as potential licensing or trademark issues.
It does not discuss the impact of these removals on OpenCode users or developers relying on Anthropic integrations.
There is no coverage of any response or statement from Anthropic or OpenCode maintainers beyond the code changes.
The article does not address whether alternative solutions or replacements for Anthropic services are planned.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Anthropic's legal actions against OpenCode regarding the shutdown of a side-loaded authentication scheme. Commenters debate the legitimacy and implications of Anthropic's business decisions, the nature of the legal threat, and the broader impact on open source and API usage.

comments.author: extr
comments.text: The OpenCode guys have really surprised me in the way they've reacted to Anthropic shutting down the side-loaded auth scheme. Very petty and bitter. It's clearly just a business decision from Anthropic and a rational one at that, usage subsidization to keep people on the first party product surface is practically the oldest business move in the book and is completely valid.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a balanced perspective recognizing the business rationale behind Anthropic's decision and criticizes the reaction of OpenCode, reflecting a reasonable understanding of market practices.

comments.author: gwd
comments.text: "Legal action" means you filed a lawsuit. This looks more like someone sent a list of requested changes, backed up by an implicit or explicit threat of legal action.
comments.analysis: The comment clarifies the terminology around 'legal action,' distinguishing between actual lawsuits and legal threats, which is an important nuance in understanding the situation.

comments.author: anonym29
comments.text:
I've said it before and I'll say it again.

The people mad about this feel they are entitled to the heavily subsidized usage in any context they want, not in the context explicitly allowed by the subsidizer.

It's kind of like a new restaurant started handing out coupons for "90% off", wanting to attract diners to the restaurant, customers started coming in and ordering bulk meals then immediately packaging them in tupperware containers and taking it home (violating the spirit of the arrangement, even if not the letter of the arrangement), so the restaurant changed the terms on the discount to say "limited to in-store consumption only, not eligible for take-home meals", and instead of still being grateful that they're getting food for 90% off, the cheapskate customers are getting angry that they're no longer allowed to exploit the massive subsidy however they want.

Anthropic has every right to place rules around their generous subsidization of the Claude subscription plans, which give limits of ~8-12x as many tokens as you'd get for the same expenditure in the PAYG API.

That said, demanding an open source repo remove information that Anthropic openly publishes and distributes for free (the prompt) is a bit odd...
comments.analysis: This comment uses an analogy to explain the rationale behind Anthropic's restrictions, emphasizing the legitimacy of setting terms for subsidized services while noting some oddities in the demand to remove publicly available prompts.

comments.author: jryio
comments.text:
Businesses exercise power and control in the market. The purpose of this is to set a precedent (perceived or actual) — the auth system was a product, not an API. Anthropic is drawing the line between 'built on us' and 'built around us.'

I don't necessarily see this as an evil action. It doesn't inhibit open source, it sets terms of service and practice boundaries.

Granted this is a wildly unpopular approach, worse has happened in the OSS world...
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully discusses the business strategy behind Anthropic's actions, highlighting the distinction between different types of integrations and the setting of boundaries.

comments.author: droidjj
comments.text: It's not clear what exactly the "legal action" is based on this github link. My pure speculation is Anthropic's lawyers have come up with a liability story boiling down to OpenCode helping end users violate the Anthropic ToS (i.e. tortious interference with contract).
comments.analysis: This comment offers a plausible legal theory regarding the basis of Anthropic's claims, though it admits to speculation due to lack of clear information.

comments.author: valunord
comments.text: This wouldn't be so bad if they didn't have such a sucky tui or ecosystem. AI is chef's kiss, tooling is bottom barrel.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses frustration about the tooling ecosystem but does not contribute meaningful analysis to the legal or business aspects of the discussion.

comments.author: jitl
comments.text: Same thing happened with Google, but Google started actually banning user's accounts. Hopefully it doesn't come to that with Anthropic.
comments.analysis: This comment draws a relevant comparison to similar actions by another company, providing context but lacking detailed evidence about Anthropic's future actions.

comments.author: cedws
comments.text: Under what law can Anthropic force OpenCode to do this? Surely it's not illegal to publish code that interacts with an API that's open for everyone to see?
comments.analysis: This comment raises a valid legal question about the enforceability of Anthropic's demands, highlighting uncertainty around the legal grounds for forcing code removal.

comments.author: strideashort
comments.text: Can anyone explain what’s going on here? Using API is illegal? that can’t possibly be since we now know API is not even copyrighted (which personally I disagree with bit whatever)… so what is going on here?
comments.analysis: This comment reflects confusion about the legal status of API usage, correctly noting that APIs generally are not copyrighted, thus questioning the basis of Anthropic's claims.

comments.author: mellosouls
comments.text:
This and threatening OpenClaw (now at OpenAI), Anthropic really on a roll making friends in Open Source.

Previously discussed I think:

Anthropic Explicitly Blocking OpenCode (173 points, 157 comments)

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46625918
comments.analysis: This comment sarcastically criticizes Anthropic's approach to open source projects but lacks substantive evidence or constructive insight.
